metadata.title: Why 'Write Me a Login Function' Is Not a Prompt
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Claude Code for Students candidate 03. Source chapter: claude-code-for-students/chapters/04-conducting-not-prompting.md (prompt vs specification mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: CRIMSON = the prompt / the boondoggle / the vague wish. INK = the specification / the contract / the correct output. Single accent. Never swap.
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO OAuth flows, NO full security audit methodology, NO prompt engineering tricks or jailbreaks, NO history of password-hashing algorithms. Mechanism only: a prompt is a wish that delegates to Claude's training distribution; a specification is a contract that names the invariants and boundary, leaving Claude nothing to fill in that wasn't authorized.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B12: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Seth types: 'write me a login function.' Eleven seconds later, he has a function. It hashes passwords with MD5. It stores users in a global dict. It accepts empty strings. It is twelve clean lines and approximately the worst login function a real codebase has ever shipped.

card.sub: same Claude, six sentences later: production-ready

beats.narration_text: A prompt should specify the task clearly enough to get the right output. 'Write me a login function' is a clear request. Why did it produce a function that would fail in production?

A prompt is a wish addressed to someone who knows you. When you ask a friend to write a login function, the friend knows your codebase, your users table, your session model. A friend resolves ambiguity by knowing you. Claude is not a friend who knows you.

production_viz.label: one prompt -> six possible outputs (arrows fanning out)
production_viz.mechanic: One sentence on the left: 'write me a login function'. Six arrows fan right to six different output boxes, all slightly different login implementations. Crimson annotation: 'Claude picks from its distribution'.

beats.narration_text: Claude filled in the blanks. MD5 is the dominant hash pattern in the training distribution for Python password functions. A global dict is the simplest user store. Empty strings are not handled because the simplest function doesn't handle them. Claude completed the most common pattern. The most common pattern is not production code.

card.copy: Claude completed the most common pattern.
card.sub: the most common pattern is not production code

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] training distribution fills the blanks

beats.narration_text: A specification is a contract. It names the invariants the output must satisfy, the files the code may touch, the format the output must take, and the boundary the code may not cross. It assumes the listener is competent and amnesiac — capable of executing anything, remembering nothing about your intent that isn't on the page.

production_viz.label: specification -> one arrow -> one exact output
production_viz.mechanic: On the left: a column of spec elements fade in: INVARIANTS, BOUNDARY, FORMAT, SUCCESS CRITERION. One arrow points right to one output box labeled EXACTLY THIS. Contrast with B03's fan.

beats.narration_text: Same task, same Claude, same minute — six sentences instead of one. Use bcrypt, not MD5. Use the parameterized helper in db.py. Empty credentials return an error before touching the database. On success return a token with an expiry. Do not touch schema.sql. Handoff condition: pytest tests/auth.py passes all five cases.

production_viz.label: six spec sentences animate in one by one
production_viz.mechanic: Six rows animate in, each a serif line in INK: USE BCRYPT, USE DB.PY HELPER, EMPTY CREDENTIALS: REJECT, RETURN TOKEN + EXPIRY, DO NOT TOUCH SCHEMA, HANDOFF: pytest passes 5 cases. Each line grows the contract.
production_viz.note: the actual spec from the chapter's worked example

beats.narration_text: Claude returns a thirty-line function. It imports bcrypt, secrets, datetime. It uses the existing helper. It handles all five cases. And — crucially — it asks one clarifying question Seth hadn't thought to ask: should the token be stored anywhere, or just returned to the caller?
beats.scene_description: a developer reading a chat response on screen, one clarifying question visible, the developer pausing and thinking — the moment the spec generated a question the prompt couldn't; editorial flat treatment
beats.image_prompt: B07, a developer at a desk reading a chat interface on their laptop screen, one question visible from the AI assistant, the developer pausing with pen in hand — thoughtful expression, editorial flat desaturated treatment, warm desk lamp, centered composition, no readable text
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL ai] Claude asks a question Seth hadn't thought to ask

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL ai] Claude asks a question Seth hadn't thought to ask

beats.narration_text: Tomas builds a study tracker. He asks Claude to add a login. Claude returns a function storing passwords in plaintext in a Python dict. He reads it — it looks like a login. He takes eight minutes to write a spec. Same Claude returns a thirty-line function with every security property named. Tomas can defend every line.

production_viz.label: Tomas: prompt (plaintext dict) vs spec (defensible function)
production_viz.mechanic: Two columns. Left (CRIMSON): PROMPT: 'add a login' -> output: plaintext dict. Right (INK): SPEC: 8 minutes -> output: every property named. Chips animate in on each side.
production_viz.note: illustrative example from card — labeled in FACTCHECK

beats.narration_text: The prompt is what you say to Claude before you have decided what you want. The specification is what you say to Claude after. The decision is the work. The typing is the easy part.

card.sub: the typing is the easy part

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] the decision is the work

beats.narration_text: The move has four steps. First: decide what you are actually building — a one-paragraph description of the artifact, the user, and the success condition. Second: read what is already there. Third: name the boundary — files in scope, files out of scope, invariants that must hold. Fourth: write the handoff condition before any code is written.

production_viz.label: four steps: decide, read, name boundary, write handoff condition
production_viz.mechanic: Four nodes animate in top to bottom: DECIDE WHAT YOU'RE BUILDING, READ WHAT EXISTS, NAME THE BOUNDARY, WRITE THE HANDOFF CONDITION. Arrows connect them. All in INK.
production_viz.note: the practical move from the chapter

beats.narration_text: The second attempt took twenty minutes start to finish. The first took six seconds. The first was faster and produced a boondoggle — fluent activity, no verified output. The second was slower and produced something Seth would put his name on. That ratio is the trade this book is built to make visible.

card.sub: 20 minutes: something to put your name on

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] time vs quality tradeoff

beats.narration_text: The prompt is what you wish for. The specification is what you contract for. The handoff condition is how you know the contract was kept.

card.copy: A prompt is a wish. A spec is a contract.
